class_schedule.topic: What Is Psychology? History and Approaches
learning_objectives: Define psychology as the scientific study of behavior and mental processes
Trace the historical development of psychology from philosophy to modern science
Compare and contrast major psychological perspectives
readings: APA Guidelines for the Undergraduate Psychology Major

lecture_notes.introduction: Psychology is the scientific study of behavior and mental processes. This course provides a broad survey of the entire discipline, from biological foundations to social influences.
key_concepts: Empiricism and the scientific method in psychology
Structuralism (Wundt, Titchener) and functionalism (James)
Behaviorism, psychoanalysis, humanism, and cognitive revolution
Modern integrative approaches: biopsychosocial model
Subfields of psychology: clinical, developmental, cognitive, social, I/O
lecture_notes.demonstration: Interactive timeline activity mapping key figures and schools of thought
setup_requirements: Access to university LMS for readings

review.value: Integrates biological, psychological, and social-cultural factors to explain behavior
Systematic observation, hypothesis testing, and replication form the backbone of psychological inquiry

class_schedule.topic: Research Methods and Ethics in Psychology
learning_objectives: Distinguish between descriptive, correlational, and experimental research designs
Identify independent and dependent variables in experiments
Explain ethical principles governing psychological research

lecture_notes.introduction: Psychological knowledge rests on rigorous methodology. Understanding how research is conducted is essential for evaluating claims about behavior.
key_concepts: Case studies, naturalistic observation, and surveys
Correlation does not imply causation
Experimental design: control groups, random assignment, double-blind procedures
Informed consent, debriefing, and IRB oversight
Replication crisis and open science movement
lecture_notes.demonstration: Class designs a simple experiment: effect of sleep on test performance, identifying variables and controls

review.value: Ensures each participant has an equal chance of being in any condition, minimizing confounds
Participants must be told about the study's purpose, procedures, and risks before agreeing to participate

learning_objectives: Describe the structure and function of neurons
Explain synaptic transmission and the role of neurotransmitters
Identify major brain structures and their functions

lecture_notes.introduction: All behavior originates in the nervous system. Understanding neurons, neurotransmitters, and brain anatomy provides the biological foundation for the rest of the course.
key_concepts: Neuron structure: dendrites, soma, axon, myelin sheath
Action potential and the all-or-none principle
Key neurotransmitters: dopamine, serotonin, GABA, acetylcholine, glutamate
Brain imaging: fMRI, PET, EEG
Cerebral cortex lobes and subcortical structures
lecture_notes.demonstration: Interactive brain anatomy model identifying regions and predicting deficits from lesions
setup_requirements: 3D brain anatomy software or physical model

review.value: Chemical messengers that cross the synaptic gap; imbalances linked to psychological disorders
Left hemisphere specializes in language and logic; right hemisphere in spatial and creative processing

learning_objectives: Describe the stages of sleep and theories of dreaming
Evaluate the effects of psychoactive substances on consciousness

lecture_notes.introduction: Our experience of the world is constructed by the brain from raw sensory input. This session explores how we sense, perceive, and experience consciousness.
key_concepts: Absolute and difference thresholds
Gestalt principles of perceptual organization
Top-down vs. bottom-up processing
Circadian rhythms and sleep architecture (NREM stages, REM)
Psychoactive drug categories: depressants, stimulants, hallucinogens
lecture_notes.demonstration: Optical illusions gallery demonstrating Gestalt principles and perceptual constancies

review.value: Proximity, similarity, closure, continuity -- the brain organizes sensory input into meaningful wholes
Rapid eye movement stage associated with vivid dreaming and memory consolidation

learning_objectives: Compare classical conditioning, operant conditioning, and observational learning
Describe the multi-store model of memory and encoding strategies
Explain basic cognitive processes: thinking, problem-solving, and language

lecture_notes.introduction: Learning and memory are central to adaptation. This session covers the major learning paradigms and how information is encoded, stored, and retrieved.
key_concepts: Pavlov's classical conditioning: acquisition, extinction, generalization
Skinner's operant conditioning: reinforcement schedules, shaping
Bandura's social learning theory and modeling
Atkinson-Shiffrin model: sensory, short-term, long-term memory
Heuristics, algorithms, and cognitive biases in decision-making
lecture_notes.demonstration: Demonstration of classical conditioning using taste aversion scenario; memory span test with chunking

review.value: Behavior is shaped by its consequences: reinforcement increases behavior, punishment decreases it
Memory retrieval is most effective when the context at retrieval matches the context at encoding

learning_objectives: Explain how psychological disorders are classified using the DSM-5
Describe symptoms and causes of anxiety, mood, and psychotic disorders
Compare biomedical and psychotherapeutic treatment approaches

lecture_notes.introduction: Psychological disorders affect millions worldwide. Understanding their classification, etiology, and treatment is essential for any psychology student.
key_concepts: DSM-5 categorical approach and dimensional alternatives
Generalized anxiety disorder, panic disorder, phobias, OCD
Major depressive disorder and bipolar disorder
Schizophrenia spectrum: positive and negative symptoms
CBT, psychodynamic therapy, humanistic therapy, and pharmacotherapy
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study analysis: identifying symptoms, formulating a diagnosis, and proposing a treatment plan

review.value: Disorders arise from the interaction of biological vulnerability (diathesis) and environmental stressors
Cognitive-behavioral therapy targets maladaptive thought patterns and behaviors; strong empirical support for anxiety and depression

quizzes.topic: Foundations of Psychology and Research Methods

questions.question: A researcher finds a correlation of r = -0.78 between hours of social media use and GPA. Can the researcher conclude that social media use causes lower grades? Explain your reasoning.
questions.answer: No. Correlation does not imply causation. The negative correlation indicates that higher social media use is associated with lower GPA, but a third variable (e.g., poor time management) could explain both, or the direction of causality could be reversed (students with lower GPAs may turn to social media). Only an experiment with random assignment can establish causation.

rubric.key: States correlation does not equal causation
Identifies possible third variables or reverse causality
Notes that only experiments establish causation

questions.question: Describe two ethical principles that an Institutional Review Board (IRB) would enforce in a study involving human participants, and explain why each matters.
questions.answer: Informed consent: participants must understand the study's purpose, procedures, risks, and their right to withdraw at any time. This respects autonomy and protects against coercion. Debriefing: after the study, participants must be told the full purpose, especially if deception was used. This restores trust and ensures no lasting harm from the research experience.

questions.question: Trace the path of a neural impulse from one neuron to the next, explaining the role of neurotransmitters at the synapse.
questions.answer: An action potential travels down the presynaptic neuron's axon to the terminal buttons. The depolarization causes synaptic vesicles to release neurotransmitter molecules into the synaptic cleft. These molecules bind to receptor sites on the postsynaptic neuron's dendrites, producing either an excitatory or inhibitory postsynaptic potential. Excess neurotransmitter is removed by reuptake or enzymatic degradation.

rubric.key: Describes action potential reaching terminal buttons

questions.question: A child touches a hot stove and pulls away. Later, the child avoids the stove entirely. Identify the type of learning involved in the avoidance behavior and explain the reinforcement principle at work.
questions.answer: The avoidance behavior is an example of operant conditioning, specifically negative reinforcement. The child's avoidance behavior (staying away from the stove) is strengthened because it removes or prevents an aversive stimulus (pain from the burn). The initial pain also involves classical conditioning: the sight of the stove (CS) becomes associated with pain (US), eliciting fear (CR).

rubric.key: Identifies operant conditioning and negative reinforcement
Explains how avoidance removes aversive stimulus

major_assignments.title: Psychological Perspectives Analysis Paper
major_assignments.description: Select a real-world behavior (e.g., addiction, aggression, altruism) and analyze it from at least four major psychological perspectives: biological, cognitive, behavioral, and sociocultural. Synthesize the perspectives into a coherent biopsychosocial explanation.
objectives: Demonstrate understanding of multiple psychological perspectives
Apply theoretical frameworks to a concrete behavior
Synthesize competing explanations into an integrated analysis
Write in APA format with proper citations

criteria: Applies perspectives correctly to the chosen behavior
Identifies strengths and limitations of each perspective
Identifies where perspectives complement or conflict
Comprehensive analysis from four or more perspectives
Professional APA-formatted paper with clear argumentation

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of reading comprehension and core concepts

participation_and_discussion.description: Active engagement in class discussions and in-class activities

midterm_examination.description: Comprehensive exam covering first half of course material

final_examination.description: Cumulative final exam covering all course material

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance is expected. More than 3 unexcused absences will result in a 5% grade deduction per additional absence.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. Extensions may be granted with documentation of extenuating circumstances provided at least 48 hours in advance.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of concepts is encouraged, but all written work must be original. Plagiarism detection software is used on all submissions.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must adhere to the university honor code. Plagiarism, cheating, or fabrication of data will result in a failing grade and referral to the academic integrity board.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to university LMS, word processing software, and APA citation tools. Laptops permitted for note-taking but not during exams.
